                      AND EARNINGS
                                                             VOL.IB NO.1& JUNE 1S72

                      Joseph M. Finerty, Editor
                      Gloria P. Green, Associate Editor
                      James A. McCall, Associate Editor

                                Editors' Note

                                    Employment and Earnings, United States, 1909-71, BLS Bulletin 1312-8 has just been
                                released by the Bureau of Labor Statistics. Based on the establishment survey, the bul-
                                letin contains historical data at the national level on industry employment, hours, earn-
                                ings, and labor turnover. All series are shown from the earliest date available through
                                May 1971. Data have been adjusted to March 1970 benchmarks and are comparable to
                                data appearing in Employment and Earnings beginning with the September 1971 issue
                                (Vol. 18 No. 3). It may be purchased for $5.00 per copy from any of the BLS regional
                                offices. A microfiche edition is also available for $.95. (See page 48 for details on
                                ordering.)

                 Job vacancies                                                           labor turnover, reprinted from the Handbook of Methods,
                                                                                         BLS Bulletin 1711—which are available upon request.
                      Job vacancies are the stock of unfilled job openings as of the
                  close of the last business day of the reference month. Openings
                  for all kinds of positions, classifications and employment, full       Size and regional stratification
                 time, part time, permanent, temporary, and seasonal are in-
                 cluded. Excluded are jobs to be filled by recall from layoff,               A number of industries are stratified by size of establish-
                 transfer, promotion, demotion or return from paid or unpaid             ment and/or by region, and the stratified production- or non-
                 leave; jobs unoccupied because of labor-management disputes;            supervisory-worker data are used to weight the hours and earn-
                 job openings for which "new" workers were already hired and             ings into broader industry groupings. Accordingly, the basic esti-
                 scheduled to start work later; and openings with future starting        mating cell for an employment, hours, or earnings series, as the
                 dates, which are requested as a separate item.                          term is used in the summary of computational methods, may be
                     Job vacancies are defined as vacant jobs which are immedi-          a whole industry or a size stratum, a region stratum, or a size
                 ately available for filling, and for which the firm is actively try-    stratum of a region within an industry.
                 ing to find or recruit workers from outside the firm.
                      "Actively trying to find or recruit" means that the establish-
                 ment is engaged in current efforts to fill the job vacancies by         Benchmark adjustments
                 means of orders listed with public or private employment agen-              Employment estimates are compared periodically with com-
                 cies and school placement offices; notification to labor unions         prehensive counts of employment which provide "benchmarks"
                 and professional organizations; "help wanted" advertising (news-        for the various nonagricultural industries, and appropriate ad-
                 paper, posted notice, etc.) recruitment programs; and interview         justments are made as indicated. The industry estimates are
                 and selection of applicants.                                            currently projected from March 1970 levels. Normally, bench-
                     Long-term job vacancies are those current vacancies which           mark adjustments are made annually.
                 have continued unfilled for 30 days or more.                                The primary sources of benchmark information are employ-
                      The reporting establishment is also asked to indicate the          ment data, by industry, compiled quarterly by State agencies
                 number of openings with future starting dates for which the firm        from reports of establishments covered under State unemploy-
                 is actively trying to recruit workers from outside the firm.            ment insurance laws. These tabulations, covering three-fourths of
                      Job openings with future starting dates may exist for such         the total nonagricultural employment in the United States, are
                 reasons as: Job unavailable until expected separation of present        prepared under the direction of the Manpower Administration.
                 incumbent occurs; work will not start until some future date;           Benchmark data for the residual are obtained from the records
                 new branch to be opened in the future; or anticipated increase in       of the Social Security Administration, the Interstate Commerce
                 business.                                                               Commission, and a number of other agencies in private industry
                      The job vacancy rate is computed by dividing the number of         or government.
                 current job vacancies by the sum of employment plus vacancies,

                 and multiplying that quotient by 100.
                                                                                         pared with new benchmark levels, industry by industry. If re-
                      Occupational classifications are made in accordance with
                                                                                         visions are necessary, the monthly series of estimates are ad-
                 those established in the Dictionary of Occupational Titles, Third
                                                                                         justed between the new benchmark and the preceding one, and
                 Edition, U.S. Department of Labor, 1965.
                                                                                         the new benchmark for each industry is then carried forward
                                                                                         progressively to the current month by use of the sample trends.
                                                                                         Thus, under this procedure, the benchmark is used to establish
                 ESTIMATING METHODS                                                      the level of employment; the sample is used to measure the
